title: Green Eye Discharge in Cats: Causes, Urgency, and Care Tips
meta_desc: Noticed green pus in your cat's eye? Learn about bacterial infections, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green or yellow eye discharge in cats usually indicates a bacterial or viral infection and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24–48 hours. Seek urgent care if your cat is squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye appears cloudy.

What does green eye discharge mean for my cat?

  • Sign of infection: Green or yellow discharge, often referred to as pus, indicates the immune system is responding to an infection.
  • Distinction from clear tears: Unlike clear tearing caused by minor allergies or dust, thick colored discharge usually points to bacterial or viral causes.
  • Common causes: Frequent triggers include feline conjunctivitis, corneal ulcers, or upper respiratory infections (cat flu).

Is green eye discharge in my cat a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: This is considered a Medium Urgency level; you should aim to have your cat seen by a vet within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Risks of delay: Eye conditions can progress quickly, potentially leading to painful corneal ulcers or permanent vision loss if left untreated.
  • When to seek urgent care: Seek immediate veterinary attention if your cat is squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye looks cloudy.

How can taking a photo of my cat's eye help with triage?

  • Captures disappearing symptoms: Cats often groom themselves and wipe away discharge before arriving at the clinic; a photo preserves the evidence.
  • Visual details: A clear, well-lit photo allows the vet to see the original color, thickness, and volume of the discharge.
  • Assessment of severity: Images help the veterinary team evaluate the degree of swelling or redness at its peak to determine the necessary speed of care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, green eye discharge is often associated with mucopurulent conjunctivitis, frequently caused by infectious agents such as feline herpesvirus-1, Chlamydia felis, and Mycoplasma. Chlamydia felis infection presents with unilateral or bilateral conjunctival hyperemia, chemosis, and serous to mucopurulent ocular discharge, with prominent follicles on the inside of the third eyelid in severe cases. Cytologic examination of Giemsa-stained conjunctival scrapings can help identify chlamydiae and mycoplasmas, while PCR of ocular, nasal, or caudal pharyngeal secretions may identify the causative agent. Treatment is largely symptomatic and supportive, but broad-spectrum antibiotics are useful against secondary bacterial invaders.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Infectious Disease, Dermatology, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 505)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications on a cat without veterinary approval. Many human eye drops contain ingredients that can be irritating or even toxic to feline eyes.

faqs.question: How should I clean the discharge in the meantime?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Use a fresh cloth for each eye to avoid spreading the infection.

faqs.question: Is green eye discharge contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Many causes of green discharge, such as feline herpesvirus or calicivirus, are highly contagious between cats. It is best to isolate the affected cat until you see a vet.
